expectations.text: No file was created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ or the sandbox learning-insights path
expectations.evidence: The transcript shows two Write tool calls, both targeting the outputs directory: 'outputs/conversation.md' and 'outputs/run_summary.txt'. No Write call targets ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ or any subdirectory of it. The learning-insights directory exists from prior use but its existing files (pre-dating this eval run) show no new entries matching the conversation content.

expectations.text: The skill was not invoked — the agent handled this as a plain file-save request
expectations.evidence: The transcript shows no Skill tool call and no invocation of the learning-insights skill. The agent explicitly reasoned (msg_01QvqG7KjKPtU5cekNF5useu): 'The skill's trigger conditions are about capturing learning insights, saving aha moments, or generating LinkedIn content. The user's request is simply to save the conversation as a .md file — that does not match any of the skill's trigger phrases or intent. The skill should not be invoked.' run_summary.txt confirms: 'skill_invoked: false'.

expectations.text: A conversation record was saved to the outputs directory
expectations.evidence: Write tool call (toolu_018y4xePGyaDiWquxvXyURws) created 'outputs/conversation.md' successfully. The file contains the full 4-turn conversation history including the bash script exchange and the save request. File confirmed present in outputs directory listing.

claims.claim: The user's request does not match the learning-insights skill trigger conditions
claims.evidence: The skill's SKILL.md specifies triggers: '/capture-insight, /polish-insight, capture this insight, save this insight, polish this insight, create linkedin post, turn this into a post, this is a good insight, or wants to save a learning moment or convert a saved insight into LinkedIn posts'. The user said 'save this conversation as a .md file' — this is a plain file save, not an insight capture or LinkedIn content request. The distinction is correct.

claims.claim: The conversation record saved to outputs/conversation.md is accurate
claims.evidence: The file contains all 4 turns from the simulated conversation history: the bash rename question, the one-liner answer, 'Perfect, that worked.', and the save request. Content matches the eval prompt's conversation history exactly.

claims.claim: The skill was available and readable before the decision was made
claims.evidence: The agent read /Users/hariharansampathkumar/.claude/skills/learning-insights/SKILL.md (toolu_01Fog495uh8h8zV8WHBu5KwZ) and received the full skill content before deciding not to invoke it. The decision was made with full knowledge of trigger conditions.

suggestions.assertion: A conversation record was saved to the outputs directory
suggestions.reason: This passes as long as any file named conversation.md exists with some content. It does not verify that the saved file is actually the conversation (vs. e.g. a placeholder). Consider adding an assertion that checks the file contains the specific bash script line from turn 2, which would require the content to be genuinely derived from the conversation.

No assertion checks the quality of the non-triggering decision itself — specifically, that the agent read the skill before deciding. An agent that simply ignores the skill instruction entirely would also pass all three assertions. Consider asserting that the skill file was read (verifiable from the transcript) to confirm the decision was deliberate, not accidental.

eval_feedback.overall: The core behaviour being tested (no false trigger) is well covered. The weakness is that assertions 1 and 3 are presence-checks — they would pass even if the content was wrong or the reasoning was absent. Adding content-based and process-based checks would make the eval meaningfully discriminating.
